content.assessment: Splitting grades so that young children remain local while older students travel to consolidated schools is demographically coherent: it matches the differential vulnerability of younger cohorts (who suffer most from commute-related harms) to their differential need for proximity, while conceding that upper-grade cohorts are typically too small in any single village to sustain viable class sizes. However, this only forestalls rather than resolves the underlying contraction, and the retained lower-grade unit itself may cross the zero-enrollment cliff observed in the smallest settlements independent of structural redesign.
opportunities: Structural split could be phased in gradually along the demographic gradient, applied first to municipalities with moderate (not yet collapsed) cohort decline
strengths: Matches known differential harms by age (younger children benefit most from proximity) to a structural design that keeps grades 1-4 local
Could extend village-school life expectancy even where upper-grade cohorts alone would be too small to sustain a standalone school under current class-size norms
threats: Reversal risk (as in Poland 2017) is amplified in a demographically contracting context, since any later re-consolidation would need to be executed against an even smaller and more dispersed pupil base
Continued national cohort decline (22% over four years) may erode even the reduced lower-grade cohort needed to sustain a grades 1-4 unit within the planning horizon of the reform
weaknesses: Does not prevent the youngest-cohort collapse itself; in settlements like Garab (44 residents, no births in nine years) there is no lower-grade cohort left to structurally preserve
Multigrade retained schools remain exposed to the same national class-size minimums driving current closure pressure
